B.title: Section B — Short Answer I (2 marks each)

questions.q: Write 715 in Roman numerals.
Write 50 in base-5.
questions.hint: Divide by 5 repeatedly; read remainders bottom to top

questions.q: What is MMCMXCIX in decimal? Show your working.
questions.hint: Subtractive rule: smaller value before larger means subtract

questions.q: Name the three symbols of the Mayan number system and their values.
questions.ans: Dot (•) = 1; Horizontal bar (—) = 5; Shell shape = 0 (zero). The system is base-20.
questions.hint: Three symbols: one for 1, one for 5, one for 0

questions.q: Who were the Oksapmin people and how did they count?
questions.ans: The Oksapmin are from Papua New Guinea. They used 27 body parts for counting — starting from the thumb of one hand, going up the arm, across the face, and down to the other hand.

questions.hint: Divide by 5 repeatedly and read remainders from bottom to top

questions.hint: Divide by 2 repeatedly and read remainders from bottom to top

questions.q: Write 302 and 1222 in Roman numerals.
questions.hint: Break each number into thousands, hundreds, tens, units

D.title: Section D — Long Answer (5 marks each)

questions.q: Compare the Egyptian, Roman, and Hindu-Arabic number systems. Which is most advanced? Give reasons.
questions.ans: Egyptian: additive base-10 hieroglyphic system; no zero; no place value; needs many symbols. Roman: additive/subtractive; seven symbols (I,V,X,L,C,D,M); no zero; not positional; arithmetic is cumbersome. Hindu-Arabic: place-value system (base-10); has zero; only 10 symbols (0–9); all arithmetic easy. Hindu-Arabic is MOST ADVANCED: (1) zero included, (2) place value means position decides the digit's value, (3) only 10 symbols represent any number, (4) arithmetic operations are efficient.
questions.hint: Compare on: zero, place value, number of symbols needed

questions.q: Trace the journey of Hindu numerals from India to the rest of the world. Name all key people and dates.
questions.ans: 1. Origin: Brahmi numerals in ancient India. 2. Bakshali Manuscript (3rd century CE): zero used as dot placeholder. 3. Brahmagupta (628 CE): Brahmasphuta-siddhanta — formally defined zero as number; rules for negative numbers. 4. Al-Khwarizmi (~820 CE): 'On the Calculation with Hindu Numerals' — spread system to Arab world; his name gave 'algorithm'. 5. Fibonacci (1202 CE): Liber Abaci — introduced Hindu-Arabic numerals to Europe. Arabs called them 'Al Hind' numerals; Europeans called them 'Arabic'. Correct name: Hindu-Arabic Numerals.
questions.hint: India to Arab world to Europe, via Brahmagupta, Al-Khwarizmi, Fibonacci
